entry.title: Re: In Manage Production Orders, the column “Order Text”...
entry.content:
Good day @Jana_Hauck 

Thank you for your question.

The Order Text column in Manage Production orders is not filled from production order header long text, the operation long text, or other maintained long text section. It refers to the production order short description/order text field exposed as ManufacturingOrderText.

If the Order Text column is empty, check whether the short order description/order text is maintained on the production order. Maintaining only header long text or order long text will not automatically populate this list column. Note that manufacturing order long text is a separate text field from ManufacturingOrderText. Basically, saying that they are separate data sources.

See the following references for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/8a8c0ffa65b14ca0aaf9678ed5b2b44c.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/3df782bd3a824e948825154000602c53.html?locale=en-US

For capacity planning, there is no standard setting to show production order long text or shift notes directly as planning table columns. You can create shift notes with reference to objects such as production orders, but they are handled through the shift note function are not automatically displayed as order text in capacity planning apps.

I suggest you test one production order by maintaining the short order description, refreshing Manage Production Orders and checking whether the Order Text column is filled.  

Then test the same order in the capacity planning app and check the available column settings. 

 

 If your business requires production order long text or shift notes directly in the planning table, this should be raised as an improvement request or handled through a custom reporting approach using released CDS views where available.

Here is more reference for more guidance

3491464 - Element which exists in the underlying custom CDS view is not selectable in Fields Selections of custom analytical query - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Mass Upload of Equipment Classification & Characteristics Data Using LTMC in SAP
entry.content:
During one of my SAP PM data migration activities, I had a requirement to upload thousands of equipment classification records into SAP. The equipment masters were already available in the system, but the challenge was assigning the correct class and characteristic values to each equipment in bulk. Initially, I thought of maintaining it manually by LSMW in IE02, but it quickly became difficult because of the huge volume of records. That is when I explored the standard LTMC template available in SAP S/4HANA and found it more efficient and reliable approach for this requirement.

In this blog, I would like to share how we used LTMC to upload equipment class and characteristic values, and a few important points that helped me complete the migration successfully.

USE CASE OVERVIEW

For this requirement, I had to upload classification data for Fleet Equipment records that were already created in SAP. Each equipment needed to be assigned to the class, and characteristics are assigned to it.

The Characteristics are:

  1. Capacity
  2. Power

Let us take the sample record used during the upload below.

Equipment

Class Type

Class

Characteristic1

Value

Characteristic2

Value

Heavy Vehicle1

002

Tipper Truck

Capacity

30 ton

Power

220kW

Heavy Vehicle2

002

Tractor

Capacity

40 ton

Power

300kW

Heavy Vehicle3

002

Crane

Capacity

120 ton

Power

350 kW

PREREQUISITES:

Before starting the upload through Migration Cockpit, a few master data and configuration activities must already be completed in the system. Ensuring these prerequisites beforehand helped avoid most of the validation errors during the upload process. Below are the main prerequisites required for uploading equipment classification data using LTMC.

  1. Equipment Master
  2. Characteristics Created
  3. Class Created

Note: Correct class type should be used. In this example, Class Type 002 was used for Equipment Classification.

MIGRATION COCKPIT PROCESS

Step :1 - Open Migration Cockpit

  • Open the Migration Cockpit
  • Create a new project by providing the project name
  • After creating the project, select the migration object: “Object Classification- General template”

SAP provides a standard migration template for Classification, since the equipment masters were already available in the system

Step: 2 – Download the Template

Download the standard Excel template provided by SAP. The template contains multiple sheets. For this activity, the important sheets were related to:

  • Equipment data
  • Class assignment
  • Characteristic values

One thing I noticed during preparation was that the characteristic names in Excel must exactly match the characteristic names maintained in SAP. Otherwise, validation errors occur during upload.

Step:3 - Maintain Classification Data

There are two key areas to fill in:

i) Header Tab: Enter the equipment number along with the assigned class type and class name for each record.

ii) Numerical Value Allocation:  Enter the characteristic names and their corresponding values for each equipment. For numerical characteristics such as Capacity and Power, ensure the values are entered in the correct format as defined in the SAP characteristic configuration before proceeding to the upload step.

Step -4: Upload the File and Execute Migration

Once the Excel file was prepared, upload the file into LTMC. SAP reads the uploaded data and performs initial checks. The following five steps in the LTMC has to be followed,

Step

Action

Details

1

Upload

Upload the prepared Excel file into the Migration Cockpit.

2

Prepare

SAP reads and validates the uploaded data against system configuration.

3

Map Fields

Map the source Excel columns to the corresponding SAP target fields.

4

Simulate

Run a simulation to identify errors before actual migration.

5

Migrate

Execute the final migration to post classification data into SAP.

These Steps are particularly important as it identifies issues such as:

  • Missing classes
  • Invalid characteristics
  • Incorrect data formats
  • Equipment number issues

Note: Always test with a small batch of records first before uploading large volumes of data. This makes troubleshooting much easier.

POST MIGRATION VERIFICATION

Once the migration was completed successfully, the equipment classification data was updated automatically in SAP. After the upload, I verified the results using the transaction IE03. Under the Classification tab, the class assignment and characteristic values were updated correctly for the equipment. This confirmed that the migration was completed as expected.

CONCLUSION

Using the standard LTMC template made the equipment classification upload process much easier compared to maintaining the data manually through IE02 or LSMW. The most important learning for me during this activity was that proper preparation of Excel data plays a major role in successful migration. Even small inconsistencies in characteristic names or data types can cause upload failures. I hope this example helps anyone working on SAP PM data migration or equipment classification upload activities in SAP S/4HANA.

entry.title: Understanding SAP Standard Validation in GGB0 with Real-Time Business Scenario!
entry.content:
Hello everyone,
By hearing the word “Validation,” it is generally assumed that it is the work of an ABAP developer. However, SAP provides a standard Validation functionality that can be easily, effectively, and accurately used by Finance Consultants without requiring ABAP development support.
Following questions arises for validation at the time of postings:

  1. What is Standard Validation?
  2. How the Validation is divided into three parts?
  3. What are keys, buttons, controls and formula need to know before performing transaction code: GGB0?
  4. How to implement? Explain with one real time business requirement.
  5. What are the Key benefits of using standard validation?

    Answer for A: What is Standard Validation?

Standard validation is configured to validate the data entered at the time of posting financial entries, ensuring accuracy and correctness of the information during postings.

It helps prevent users from entering incorrect or incomplete data while posting transactions. In case any invalid data is entered, the system triggers the predefined error message as per the validation rules configured in transaction code GGB0.

Answer for B: How the Validation is divided into three parts?

In GGB0 at the time of configuration validation step is divided in to three parts:

First Part:

Following finance tables and fields under them can be used to select/create perquisite

Prerequisite in this part we have to enter which field is prerequired before checking condition in second part.

Second Part:

Below tables are given to set/create condition

In Check part as well, we have set what condition will be fulfilled and on the basis of check system knows either it has to trigger error or not.

Third Part:

Message part will be created which message will be triggered if check condition is not fulfilled.
To create message click in highlighted button

Answer for C: What are keys, buttons, controls and formula need to know before performing transaction code: GGB0?

First, we need to create validation before that need to select that it is a header data validation or line item validation, I have created line item validation.
Click on create Validation as highlighted.

Call up point 1 for Header validation and 2 for line item validation.

Second step is to assign validation to company code with activation status by
Transaction code- OB28.

ZASUS assigned to Company code-ASUS

(Note: As per company code and per call up point only validation is assigned in OB28)

Following are the activation level select Activate-1

Click on save

Perform Transaction code- GGB0 Under one validation maximum 999 steps are added and note that only one validation is created and under that steps are added as per different scenarios and conditions.

To create step select validation and click on create step as highlighted.

Double click on Prerequisite node

These button are more important to understand

=   button used for Example: Transaction code=FB60 means equals to this values
<> button used for Example: Transaction code<>FB60 means not equals to this values
(    button used for Example: (Transaction code<>FB60 means opening bracket to this values
)    button used for Example: Transaction code<>FB60) means closing bracket to this values
>= button used for Example: Posting date>=01.04.2026) means greater than and equals to this values
<= button used for Example: Posting date<=01.04.2026) means lesser than and equals to this values
> button used for Example: Posting date>01.04.2026) means greater than to this values
< button used for Example: Posting date<01.04.2026) means lesser than to this values
AND button used for Example: (Posting date<01.04.2026) AND (Transaction code= FB60)means this values both values should be there.
OR button used for Example: (Posting date<01.04.2026) OR (Transaction code= FB60)means either this value or this value any one of these will fulfilled.

Constant button is used to enter values. Example: FB60, 12.04.2026, 1501, Etc.

Delete Element is used to delete values/formulas entered.

Delete from Cursor is used to delete values/formulas entered note that it will from where the cursor is there.

Insert button will allow you to add new formula or values from where cursor is pointed.

End insertion means it will bring the cursor to end in the prerequisite.

Answer for 😧 Explain with one real time business requirement. How to implement?

Business Requirement: Need Business place and Section code field mandatory for only Transaction code: FB60, FB70, FB65, FB75, MIRO, F-53, F-28, F-47, F-48 and for company code: ASUS as it can’t controlled by field status variant. Now, we will control by validation.

Implement:

Step 01: Create validation through Transaction code- GGB0 and assign validation to Company code, activate through Transaction code- OB28 as previously we created.

Step 02: Add Prerequisite

For Adding Transaction code:

Click opening bracket & double click on SYST Table and double click on SYST-TCODE, then click on = button, click on button CONSTANT add Transaction code- FB60 press enter, select OR button, double click on SYST-TCODE, then click on = button, click on button CONSTANT add Transaction code- FB60 press enter and then follow these steps till all Transaction code will added, In the end close the bracket.

For Adding Company code:

Click on AND, then click on opening bracket, select table BKPF and double click on BKPF-BUKRS, click on = button, click on CONSTANT button and enter ASUS, close the bracket.

Step 03: Add Check

Select Table BSEG and double click on BSEG-BUPLA. Select the button <> not equals to, select CONSTANT button and enter ‘’ inverted commas as it denotes as blank.
Further select button AND, select BSEG-SECCO, select <> button not equals to, select CONSTANT button, enter values ‘’ inverted commas as it denotes as blank.

Step 04: Add Message

Go to node Message
Click on Pencil button

Create Message feed text need in error message.
I have created message no: 001

Enter Message type: E, Message number: 001
In message variable fields we have to enter table and field name which was mentioned in CHECK node.

Click on save the step

To check the overview of step 001, click on step 001
We get the all parts on one screen Prerequisite, Check and Message.

Step 05: Check validation is working or not

Select validation, click on Extras and select Activate total trace

Activate Total trace will help whether all validations are working while preforming Transaction for which validation is set.

 

Go to Transaction code: FB60

I entered the company code ASUS, vendor, posting date, invoice date and amount
Note: Not entered Business place and section code.       

Click Enter
This screen will appear as we have not entered Business place and Section code check result shows False

Click Back

Error message visible as we can see in Image

After entering Business place and Section code result is True

As our validation works and now you can create different validation steps under same validation as per different business requirement.

Answer for E: What are the Key benefits of using standard validation?

Improves data accuracy: It checks user-entered values against business rules and stops invalid postings.

Flexible configuration: Rules can be defined at header, line item, or complete document level depending on the business need.

Works in real time: Validation is triggered during document entry, so issues are caught before the document is posted.

Reduces dependency on custom coding: Many business checks can be handled through configuration rather than technical development.

Before explaining this, I would also like to share this requirement because, after understanding the validation functionality, I felt there is very limited content available in the community that explains it with a practical example/scenario. A single clear example can help consultants understand the concept easily and also assist in building similar scenarios in future.

Every Finance Consultant should have knowledge of this standard validation functionality, including how it works and how it can be used independently without ABAP development support to validate data and ensure the correctness of document postings as per business requirements.

Thanks for referring my content.
Regards,
Ronit Bhavsar
SAP FI

entry.title: SARA Retrieve
entry.content: We need to transfer data from one SAP system to another SAP system. Unfortunately, we also need to include already archived data (ADK) files in the new SAP system. Is there a way to retrieve already archived documents back into the SAP database in order to then perform the system transfer and store the files physically in the new SAP system? Please do not comment that this is a bad use case. Unfortunately, I have to examine this exact use case. And I am asking myself whether this is fundamentally possible. The SAP system is an on-premise system (2023).

entry.title: Why HR Transformation Stalls - and How the Right Expert Guidance Gets It Back on Track
entry.content:
As an HR Leader, you already know how easily momentum can slow once a new system is live and initial priorities shift. What tends to be less visible is how the nature of the work changes once the initial programme structure gives way to everyday reality. 

After go‑live, transformation stops looking like a defined project and starts behaving like a continuous series of decisions. HR teams are no longer executing against a fixed plan; they are constantly choosing what to adopt, what to defer, what to optimise, and what to leave untouched, all while continuing to run core HR operations.  

Over time, this shift places a different kind of pressure on teams, one that is less about delivery capacity and more about decision quality.  This is the context in which many HR transformations begin to lose momentum. 

Where HR Transformations Commonly Lose Momentum 

From an HR perspective, transformation rarely stalls because something is technically broken: the system functions as expected, payroll runs, and transactions are processed. What changes is the environment around the system. 

HR teams are asked to support continuing organisational change while maintaining operational stability. New business priorities emerge alongside existing commitments. Regional requirements need to be balanced with global frameworks. Regular SAP SuccessFactors releases introduce new capabilities, but also require careful judgement about relevance, timing, and adoption. AI enters conversations as both an opportunity and a source of new responsibility. 

As these pressures accumulate, HR teams often shift their focus toward maintenance. Effort goes into keeping things running smoothly, resolving exceptions, and minimising risk. Optimisation work is delayed, not because it is unimportant, but because the cost of getting decisions wrong feels higher than the benefit of moving faster. 

This is usually the moment when HR leaders recognise that the challenge is no longer the technology itself, but how decisions are being made around it. 

Why More Features Do Not Automatically Create More Value 

SAP SuccessFactors continues to evolve, and customers benefit from a regular flow of new functionality and innovation. 

However, increased capability does not automatically translate into increased value. Value in HR comes from the choices organisations make: which features to adopt, how to align processes across regions, how to manage data consistently, how to balance standardisation and flexibility, and how to introduce AI in a way that builds trust rather than uncertainty. 

When HR teams lack the time, context, or confidence to make these decisions, progress naturally slows. This slowdown should not be confused with resistance. In most cases, it is a rational response to increasing complexity and perceived risk. 

At this point, successful transformation depends less on access to features and more on access to guidance. 

The Role of Expert Guidance in Sustaining HR Transformation 

Expert guidance becomes critical precisely when transformation moves from execution into continuous navigation. 

The purpose of the HCM Services & Support portfolio is not to replace HR ownership or to manage transformation on behalf of customers. Its role is to support HR teams as transformation becomes an ongoing operating reality rather than a time‑bound programme. 

The portfolio provides structured access to SAP expertise, learning, and advisory support throughout the HR lifecycle, particularly at moments when priorities compete, complexity increases, or decisions have long‑term consequences.  

The intention is to support HR teams before issues escalate, before trade‑offs become irreversible, and before opportunities are missed. This engagement model is formalised through Success Plans. 

Success Plans: Meeting HR Where the Transformation Is Today 

Success Plans define how SAP engages with customers over time:  

  • Every SAP SuccessFactors customer begins with the Foundational Success Plan, which provides the baseline support required to operate confidently in a continuously changing cloud environment. This includes business continuity, system stability, access to enablement and learning, and preventive support as the solution evolves. As HR ambitions expand, that foundation can be extended rather than replaced. 
  • The Advanced Success Plan supports HR teams that want to move from stability toward continuous improvement. It introduces proactive innovation and adoption guidance, structured optimisation activities, and AI‑driven insights that help teams turn new capabilities into meaningful outcomes. 
  • The Max Success Plan is designed for organisations where HR transformation has become complex, multi‑year, or strategically critical. It provides deeper expert involvement and transformation‑level guidance to support global governance, architectural coherence, and long‑term decision‑making. 

Each plan builds on the one below it. HR teams do not reset their approach; they deepen it as the nature of their challenges changes. 

How Services Show Up in Everyday HR Work 

 The value of HCM Services & Support is most apparent in the situations HR teams face daily. This might be when a new release prompts questions about relevance and prioritisation, when process simplification is desirable but the implications are unclear, when data issues begin to affect confidence in analytics, or when AI enters the roadmap unevenly across regions. Often, it is when transformational goals compete with operational realities. 

In these situations, expert guidance helps HR teams move forward with greater confidence by providing context, framing decisions, and reducing uncertainty. The goal is not to accelerate change for its own sake, but to make progress manageable and deliberate. 

Beyond Guidance: When Delivery and Capacity Are Required 

Guidance alone is not always sufficient: HR transformations also require execution. For this reason, the Services & Support portfolio allows customers to complement Success Plans with Professional Services, Development Services, and Application Management Services as needed. 

Professional Services support implementation and transformation initiatives. Development Services help create organisation‑specific extensions or AI use cases. Application Management Services provide operational support when internal teams need to focus on strategic priorities. 

These services are scenario‑driven and additive. They work alongside Success Plans, giving HR teams the ability to combine guidance with delivery based on their current needs. 

From Slowed Momentum to Sustainable Progress 

HR transformation rarely stalls because of a single decision or misstep. Momentum slows when complexity begins to outpace the organisation’s ability to make confident, aligned choices. 

The HCM Services & Support portfolio exists to close that gap. It provides HR teams with the right level of guidance, expertise, and support as transformation evolves from go‑live into sustained value creation. 

For HR leaders, the most relevant question is not whether support is needed, but what kind of support will help the organisation move forward at this stage of its journey. 

That is a conversation your Customer Success Manager or Account Executive can help you explore: starting from the foundation you already have, and building forward as your HR strategy continues to evolve.

entry.title: Beyond Implementation: How SAP Professional Services Extend Your SAP SuccessFactors Capability
entry.content:
For many SAP SuccessFactors customers, “Professional Services” is a term strongly associated with the early stages of the journey: system selection, implementation, and go‑live. Once the system is live, it is easy to assume that Professional Services naturally step out of the picture and that ongoing needs are covered elsewhere. In practice, the opposite is often true. 

As HR organizations move from implementation into steady operation and continuous change, they frequently encounter moments where guidance alone is no longer sufficient. At those points, what is needed is hands-on delivery, structured execution, or additional capacity to move a specific initiative forward. This is where SAP Professional Services continue to play an important role, well beyond go‑live. 

What SAP Professional Services Are Designed to Do 

SAP Professional Services provide tailored, expert consulting and learning engagements that help customers implement, adopt, optimize, and scale their SAP Business Suite solutions, including SAP SuccessFactors. These services are standalone service offerings, available to customers at any stage of their journey through a separate services contract.  

Unlike SAP Success Plans, which define an ongoing engagement and guidance model, Professional Services are complex project‑based and execution‑focused. Each engagement has a clearly defined scope, timeline, and outcome, and is delivered as a one-time, non‑recurring SAP‑led services engagement. 

In other words, Professional Services exist to create deliverables, not just recommendations. 

Why Professional Services Matter After Go‑Live 

Once SAP SuccessFactors is live, HR transformation does not slow down, it changes shape.  Organizations begin to expand scope, respond to new business requirements, refine processes, adopt new innovations, or revisit decisions that were made under initial project constraints. These needs often require more than advisory input: they require design, configuration, integration work, data migration, testing, enablement, or structured optimization activities executed with delivery discipline. 

SAP Professional Services are explicitly designed to support this post go‑live phase, when there’s a need beyond advisory, helping customers transition from first use into continuous evolution. 

Typical scenarios include post‑go‑live optimization, expansion to new regions or user groups, data or integration rework, adoption of released innovations, or tuning the solution to better reflect evolving business priorities.  

How Professional Services Differ From Success Plans 

The distinction between Success Plans and Professional Services is intentional and important. SAP Success Plans have advisory focus with a portfolio of configuration activities that have very concrete outcomes and help to enable standard features in already live SAP SuccessFactors modules to support further changes, or updates needed. They provide guidance, assessments, reviews, reports, and recommendations. Their purpose is to help customers make better decisions and sustain adoption over time through a long-term engagement model. 

SAP Professional Services, by contrast, are execution‑focused engagements for complex requirements. They deliver end-to-end implementation and optimization services, including configuration, integration, data migration, testing, knowledge transfer, and post‑go‑live support where required. 

The two work best together, but they are not interchangeable. SAP Success Plans inform what should be done and why. Professional Services help execute how it is done and deliver tangible outcomes within agreed project boundaries. 

The Types of Professional Services HR Teams Use Most Often 

SAP Professional Services for SAP SuccessFactors are structured around clear service categories that reflect how HR needs evolve over time: 

  • Implementation Services support full end-to-end implementations or structured rollouts, whether for new deployments, conversions, upgrades, or landscape transformations. These services include the creation of project deliverables and the execution required to bring solutions into production. 
  • Optimization Services focus on the transition from go‑live into continuous evolution. They support complex enhancements driven by new business requirements, released innovations, and efficiency improvements, helping customers refine and expand their solution in a controlled way. These are usually project-based engagements that continue until the expected outcome is fully achieved.  
  • Learning Services support organizational change and adoption. They help HR teams plan and deliver enablement activities, develop learning content, and drive user acceptance as the solution evolves. 

All of these services can be delivered as fixed‑scope packaged services or tailored engagements, depending on customer needs and complexity. 

How Professional Services Fit Into the Broader Services & Support Portfolio 

SAP Professional Services are part of a broader portfolio that also includes SAP Success Plans, Development Services, and Application Management Services. 

They are designed to complement, not replace, the ongoing guidance and support provided through Success Plans. Professional Services can be used alongside any Success Plan level - Foundational, Advanced, or Max - when a customer needs structured execution to move a specific initiative forward. 

This modular approach allows HR organizations to combine long-term guidance with targeted delivery, scaling engagement up or down depending on what the situation requires. 

How Engagement Typically Starts 

Because Professional Services are delivered under a separate services contract, engagement begins with a scoping discussion. This helps clarify the business objective, define the scope and deliverables, and determine the most appropriate service approach: whether fixed‑scope or tailored, fixed‑price or time‑and‑materials.  

Seen in this light, SAP Professional Services are not simply an implementation tool. They are a way to extend your organization's capability at key moments, bringing in SAP expertise and delivery capacity when internal teams need support to move faster, reduce risk, or execute change with confidence. 

Used alongside SAP Success Plans, they help ensure that HR transformation does not stall at advice but translates into measurable progress. 

For customers, the first step is usually to connect with their Customer Success Manager, Account Executive, or Services Sales Executive. Together, you can assess whether SAP Professional Services are the right way to support your current initiative and initiate the scoping process.

entry.title: Re: The Role of Process Atoms in Modern Business Process Management
entry.content:
I am revisiting this article from a few years back, Timotheus, and your insights into the role of process atoms have aged incredibly well. You’ve hit on a critical evolution in modern BPM. As the industry shifts heavily toward data-driven analysis and Generative AI, traditional imperative models (like BPMN) simply lack the flexibility to handle dynamic variations and data-state logic. Fusing imperative context with concise declarative constraints, process atoms,  is exactly the right path forward.

At DCR Solutions, we have spent years pioneering and productizing this exact paradigm using DCR (Dynamic Condition Response) graphs.

To touch upon the challenges you raised regarding how to model these atoms for business analysts, I actually took the SAP Signavio SIGNAL query from your article and mapped it natively into a visual DCR Graph.

Instead of hiding the logic in raw query code, the business analyst simply works with the standard events. They draw a Response relation/rule from 'Close ticket' to 'Re-open ticket', and apply a data Guard. Since you rightly emphasized the importance of DMN in your post, we use FEEL (Friendly Enough Expression Language) natively for these guards, in this case, a simple [ status == 'blocked' ].

When the event is triggered, the engine evaluates the FEEL guard. If true, it instantly creates a pending response, mapping beautifully to the exact "leads-to" (∼>) behavior you described, but in a way that is immediately readable for a business user.

It is fantastic to see SAP championing the declarative approach. It validates what we see in the market every day: the future of BPM belongs to hybrid architectures that can seamlessly bridge the gap between process models, decision logic, and live data.

entry.title: The system allows a Service Entry Sheet (SES) to be reversed in a period earlier than its original p
entry.content:

This means the reversal is allowed in a previous month, prior to the original posting period.

Is this standard SAP functionality? If yes, what is the reason behind allowing such behavior, as it appears to be logically inconsistent?

entry.title: ‌🇩🇪‌ Webinar (in English): SAP Document and Reporting Compliance B2B e-Invoicing in Germany
entry.content:
Electronic invoicing requirements in Germany are evolving rapidly. Since January 2025, companies must be able to receive EN 16931-compliant e-invoices. From January 2027, organisations with annual turnover above €800,000 must issue structured e-invoices for domestic B2B transactions, with all companies included from January 2028.

SAP Document and Reporting Compliance supports these requirements, helping organisations manage compliant e-invoicing processes within their SAP landscape.

In this webinar, SAP localization experts will provide:

  • An overview of regulatory requirements and timelines
  • Key capabilities of SAP Document and Reporting Compliance
  • Insights into inbound and outbound e-invoicing processes
  • A live system demonstration

Speakers:
Chi Wan Yuen, Product Manager SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, SAP SE
Sar'a Güzelkaya, Localization Product Manager Germany, SAP SE

This session will be help in English.

Join to stay informed and prepare for upcoming compliance obligations! REGISTER NOW 

Curious about how SAP handles SAP Document Reporting Compliance in Germany? Join the Germany - SAP Document and Reporting Compliance Work Zone group and hear directly from the experts.

entry.title: Re: change of Payment format for sales(customer incoming...
entry.content:
Good day @jerrysam 

Thank you for your question.

If your requirement is to upload new bank statements and process customer payments, then you must handle the format as an incoming bank file format, not as an outgoing payment medium format. The relevance place is Map Format Data for Incoming Files from Banks, where you can create or adjust a format mapping based on an existing bank statement format.

See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/5481c611480e4c23bf49e3af651d8e4d.html?locale=en-US

After you have concluded the mapping and released it, use Test Incoming Format Mappings                 

and then upload the file in Manage Incoming Payment Files by selecting Bank Statement file type and the relevant format. 

If the uploaded file is accepted but the customer payment is not posted or cleared correctly, then the issue is no longer the file format only. Check Make Global Settings for Electronic Bank Statement, external transaction types, posting rules, account symbols and the payment transaction type group in Manage Bank Accounts.

Here are references for more guidance

3688448 - Missing Partner Name in CAMT.053.001.08 Bank Statement Reprocessing - SAP for Me

3638363 - Bank ledger not generated after bank statement import in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Driving Seamless End-to-End Real-Time GST Integration from SAP to GSTIN Using Deloitte Bolt‑on API's
entry.content:
The business requirement is to send data to the GSTIN portal in real time whenever billing is performed from the OTC side, stock movement occurs from the STP side, or accounting postings are made from the RTR side. In response, we will receive an acknowledgment in the form of an Eway Bill or eInvoice, depending on the business scenario.

We have leveraged Deloitte India’s GSTN-accredited ASP and GSP platform, Bolt‑on, to streamline and automate GST reporting, compliance, and reconciliation processes. It is integrated with SAP via middleware (PI/PO) using REST APIs.

OTC Module :

Tcodes: VF01 & VF02 & VF11

VF01 - At the creation of billing document.

Use ENHANCEMENT-SECTION     rv_invoice_document_add_02 SPOTS es_saplv60a in FM - RV_INVOICE_DOCUMENT_ADD.

At this point, the VBELN is generated, enabling easy retrieval of sales, billing, and condition record data. The Bolt‑on payload expects mandatory information to be maintained by the user within header text elements at the transaction level. In case of incorrect data entry, errors will occur. The same enhancement spot can then be utilized to correct the data and retrigger the process from the VF02 transaction.

The same enhancement spot can be leveraged for handling VF11(Cancel Billing Document).

 

Note: Do not block the process with an error message if the API response returns an error, as this may lead to number range skipping issues.

Use Include RV60AFZZ, specifically Form USEREXIT_NUMBER_RANGE, to handle all mandatory text fields and prevent missing data in the request payload.

STP module:

Tcodes : MIRO & MR8M

 

Badi Definition Name : INVOICE_UPDATE

Interface : IF_EX_INVOICE_UPDATE

IF_EX_INVOICE_UPDATE~CHANGE_AT_SAVE Use this method for all validations.

IF_EX_INVOICE_UPDATE~CHANGE_BEFORE_UPDATE Use this method for Processing & API calling logic.

The same enhancement can be leveraged for handling MR8M(Invoice cancelling).

RTR Module:

Tcodes:

FB01

FB60

FB65

FV60

FV65

FBV0

FBVB

FB70

FB75

FV70

FV75

BTE - 00001030

This BTE can be used for both document posting and document cancellation processes.

All RTR module error handling do in the Program - RGGBR000

Maintain the ABAP Proxy class with PI/PO structures for each API. For processing logic, use separate classes for each module to handle business logic, table updates, and error handling.

API details:

Tables for Update:

  1. J_1IG_INVREFNUM - GST India: Link IRN details to Document
  2. J_1IG_EWAYBILL - Link E-Way Bill to Document
  3. Build one custom table with all structure API fields to store logs - To cross verify the request payload data.
  4. One more custom table to store both EWAY & Einvoice pdf URLs.
We went live with this approach in 2023, and the solution has since stabilized, consistently handling hundreds of transactions per day.

Goods and Services Tax (GST India) SD Billing SAP S/4HANA Finance API #deloitte Bolt-on #EWAY # Einvoice

entry.title: SAP Business Network se prépare au passage vers la facturation électronique en France
entry.content:
Plusieurs pays d'Europe mettent actuellement en place des règlementations sur la facturation électronique, notamment pour des raisons de fiscalité et de visibilité des opérations entre entreprises. C’est pourquoi SAP a développé une stratégie à long terme afin de répondre à ces exigences diverses et en constante évolution.

Après la Belgique et la Pologne cette année, la France est le prochain pays à passer à la facturation électronique obligatoire.  Initialement prévue pour le 1er juillet 2024, l’obligation a été reportée à septembre 2026. À partir de cette date, l'émission de factures au format électronique et le e-reporting deviennent obligatoires pour les grandes et moyennes entreprises, tandis que la réception de factures électroniques devient obligatoire pour toutes les entreprises. Cette approche par phases donne aux organisations plus modestes le temps d'adapter leurs systèmes et processus.

Techniquement, la réforme exige des entreprises qu'elles émettent, envoient et reçoivent les factures dans l'un des formats conformes à la norme européenne : UBL, CII ou Factur-X. La France introduit également un concept de traçabilité des factures via des statuts standardisés, ce qui permet une visibilité de bout en bout pour tous les partenaires commerciaux, et un rapprochement facilité. Le fournisseur et l’acheteur sont tenus d’utiliser une plateforme privée certifiée, appelée Plateforme agréée (PA). L’obligation d’e-facturation s'applique uniquement aux factures nationales, ce qui signifie que les transactions intra-UE ou transfrontalières ne relèvent pas de la réforme.

entry.title: Auditing to Different Locations in SAP Adaptive Server Enterprise (ASE)
entry.content:
Overview

Auditing plays a vital role in maintaining database security, regulatory compliance, and operational accountability in SAP Adaptive Server Enterprise. By capturing security-related events such as login activity, privilege changes, object access, and administrative operations, the ASE audit subsystem provides administrators with a reliable mechanism for tracking and reviewing critical system activity.

Historically, SAP ASE stored audit records exclusively in the sysaudits table within the sybsecurity database. While this approach remains useful for database-centric auditing, modern enterprise environments often require tighter integration with operating system logging frameworks and centralized monitoring solutions.

To address these requirements, now SAP ASE supports multiple audit destinations through the audit trail type configuration parameter. In Linux environments, audit records can be directed to:

  • the sysaudits table,
  • the operating system syslog infrastructure,
  • or both simultaneously (Starting from ASE 16.1 SP01)

The ability to write audit records to syslog enables integration with standard Linux logging utilities such as rsyslog, making it possible to:

  • Redirect audit logs to dedicated files
  • Forward audit events to centralised log servers
  • Integrate with SIEM platforms
  • Separate ASE audit data from general system logs for easier administration and monitoring

Starting with ASE 16.1 SP01, SAP ASE introduces support for the both audit trail type, allowing audited events to be written simultaneously to both the database audit table and the OS syslog service. This provides greater flexibility for environments that require both long-term database-side retention and external log aggregation.

This blog discusses the available audit trail types in SAP ASE, explains how syslog-based auditing works on Linux, and walks through the configuration required to redirect ASE audit records to custom log files using rsyslog.

 

Understanding the audit trail type Configuration (Linux Only)

 
The audit trail type configuration parameter specifies where SAP ASE stores audit records -
 
Property Value
Default Valuetable
Valid Valuestable, syslog, both
StatusDynamic
Platform SupportLinux only (syslog and both)
Required RoleSystem Security Officer
Configuration GroupSecurity Related

 

Supported Audit Destinations

1. table (Default - All Platforms)

Audit records are written to the sysaudits table in the sybsecurity database.
This is the traditional ASE auditing mechanism and remains the default configuration.

Command to explicitly configure audit trail type to 'table' -

sp_configure "audit trail type", 0, "table"
go

2. syslog (Linux Only)

Audit records are sent to the Linux operating system syslog service.
These entries are typically visible in standard system log files such as:
/var/log/messages

Command to configure audit trail type to 'syslog' -

sp_configure "audit trail type", 0, "syslog"
go

3. both (Introduced in ASE 16.1 SP01, Linux Only)

Starting with ASE 16.1 SP01, ASE introduces a new audit trail type:

both

When enabled, audit records are written simultaneously to:

  • The sysaudits table
  • The OS syslog service

This option provides flexibility for environments that require:

  • Database-level audit retention
  • Centralized OS-level log aggregation
  • External monitoring or SIEM integration

Command to configure audit trail type to 'both' -

sp_configure "audit trail type", 0, "both"
go

Every ASE audit entry written through syslog is tagged with:

SAP_ASE_AUDIT
This tag becomes important when configuring custom log routing using rsyslog.

Configuring the Audit Trail Type

 
Since the values for audit trail type  are character data, when configuring the parameter with sp_configure  you must use some value as a placeholder for the second parameter, which must be numeric, for example:
sp_configure "audit trail type", 0, "syslog"
go

Redirecting ASE Audit Logs to a Custom File in Linux

When using the syslog or both audit trail types, ASE audit entries are processed by the Linux syslog subsystem.

Using rsyslog, administrators can redirect these audit messages to any custom file location.

This approach is useful for:

  • Separating audit logs from general system logs
  • Simplifying audit monitoring
  • Integrating with external log collectors
  • Managing dedicated audit retention policies

Step-by-Step Configuration Using rsyslog (3 Steps)

 

Step 1 — Create an rsyslog Configuration File

Create a dedicated configuration file under:

/etc/rsyslog.d/
Example:
sudo touch /etc/rsyslog.d/ase.conf

Notes

  • The filename does not need to be ase.conf.

  • rsyslog loads configuration files in alphabetical order during startup.
  • Rule ordering becomes important when using directives such as stop.

Step 2 — Add a Routing Rule

Insert the following lines into the configuration file:

if $programname == 'SAP_ASE_AUDIT' then /path_to_redirect_ase_logs
& stop
 
Example:
if $programname == 'SAP_ASE_AUDIT' then /var/log/ase_audit.log
& stop

Understanding the Rule

$programname

In rsyslog terminology:

$programname: represents the syslog tag (also called the program tag or ident) associated with the message.

SAP ASE audit messages use:

SAP_ASE_AUDIT

& stop

The directive:

& stop: terminates further processing of the current message.

Without this line, the same audit entry will continue to propagate to:

/var/log/messages
or to other matching rules.

Important Behavior of rsyslog Rule Processing

rsyslog configuration files are parsed and loaded into an ordered rule set during startup.

At runtime, each incoming log message is evaluated sequentially against these rules. Because configuration files are processed alphabetically, rule order directly affects message routing behavior.

Example — Rule Ordering Behavior

Consider the following two configuration files:

abc.conf

if $programname == 'SAP_ASE_AUDIT' then /audit1.log
& stop

xyz.conf

if $programname == 'SAP_ASE_AUDIT' then /audit2.log
& stop

Since abc.conf is processed first:

  1. The audit message matches the rule in abc.conf
  2. The message is written to /audit1.log
  3. & stop immediately halts further processing
  4. The message never reaches xyz.conf
  5. The message also does not propagate to /var/log/messages

As a result, the audit entry appears only in:

/audit1.log

Step 3 — Restart rsyslog

After updating the configuration, restart the service:

sudo systemctl restart rsyslog
 

Additional Notes

Custom Log File Locations

Audit logs can be redirected to any arbitrary file path and are not restricted to root-owned directories.

Example:

/home/ase/logs/audit.log
 

Automatic File Creation

If the target log file does not exist, rsyslog automatically creates it when the first matching message arrives.

Depending on the target location, administrators may need to configure:
  • File ownership
  • Read permissions
  • Log rotation policies

This typically requires root privileges.

When Should You Use Each Audit Trail Type?

tableTraditional ASE auditing with database-side retention
syslogCentralized OS logging or external SIEM integration
bothHybrid environments requiring both DB retention and OS-level monitoring

Conclusion

The enhanced auditing capabilities introduced in ASE 16.1 SP01 provide significantly greater flexibility for audit log management in Linux environments.

By supporting:

  • table
  • syslog
  • both

SAP ASE allows administrators to integrate database auditing more effectively into modern operational and security workflows.

Combined with Linux rsyslog routing rules, ASE audit records can be redirected, isolated, archived, and monitored using standard enterprise logging infrastructure, making audit management more scalable and operationally efficient.

entry.title: Accelerating Time to Value: How Targeted SAP Services Complement Your Internal HR Team
entry.content:
For most HR organizations, time to value means different things at different stages of the SAP SuccessFactors journey. Before go-live, the focus is often on getting the system up and running quickly, with enough standardization to support the business and enough flexibility to reflect local needs. After go-live, attention shifts to a different set of priorities: stabilizing operations, optimizing processes, expanding scope, and adopting new capabilities without overloading internal teams. 

Both phases raise resourcing questions, but what changes is how those questions are answered. 

Time‑to‑Value During Implementation: Speed, Structure, and Choice 

During initial implementation, time to value is closely tied to speed and predictability. Organizations typically want to move from contract to first use as efficiently as possible, while minimizing risk and disruption. In this phase, many customers choose to work with implementation partners, and SAP fully supports that model. Partners bring scale, local presence, industry expertise, and deep knowledge of the customer’s context, and customers retain the freedom to choose the provider that best fits their organization, geography, and delivery approach. 

SAP Professional Services complement this landscape by offering accelerated implementation packages and expert delivery options where appropriate. These include fixed scope, fixed fee services designed to drive “first use” of SAP cloud solutions and establish a stable foundation quickly. For some customers, SAP‑led implementation provides additional confidence around solution design, governance, or complex integration scenarios. For others, SAP Professional Services are used selectively alongside a partner‑led approach to strengthen specific aspects of delivery. 

The key point is that during implementation, time to value is about getting live with confidence, and customers can combine partner expertise, SAP services, and internal knowledge in the way that best suits their organization. 

The Practical reality of HR Resourcing After Go Live 

Most HR organizations are not short on capability: they understand their workforce, their policies, and their operating model better than anyone else. Once SAP SuccessFactors is live, the nature of HR work changes significantly. The challenge is rarely knowledge; it is capacity and timing. 

Post go-live HR work tends to cluster around specific moments: expanding scope to new regions, optimizing processes that were deliberately kept simple during implementation, responding to new regulatory or business requirements, or adopting capabilities introduced through regular releases. These initiatives often have defined objectives and timelines, but they cannot always justify long‑term internal resourcing, nor do they always align cleanly with partner delivery models optimized for large projects. 

In these situations, HR teams are faced with a familiar trade‑off: building everything internally preserves knowledge, but it can slow delivery and stretch teams that are already operating at full capacity. Bringing in targeted external services can accelerate progress, but only if those services are well scoped, clearly governed, and aligned with HR goals. 

This is precisely the gap SAP Professional Services are designed to address. 

What SAP Professional Services Add in a Post Go Live Context 

SAP Professional Services are designed to support customers well beyond implementation, through project‑based engagements that deliver clear outcomes within an existing SAP SuccessFactors environment. 

These engagements differ from initial implementations in an important way - they are executed in the context of what already exists. Architecture decisions, data models, integrations, and established ways of working are already in place, and Professional Services build on that reality rather than starting from a blank slate. 

Common post go-live scenarios include optimization of business processes, adoption of newly released functionality, system tuning to support changing business needs, or expansion into additional countries or user populations. These initiatives often require deep SAP expertise, structured execution, and discipline around scope, but not permanent internal resourcing or full‑scale programme delivery. 

By engaging SAP Professional Services for these initiatives, HR teams can accelerate delivery while keeping strategic ownership and operational knowledge where it belongs: internally. 

Working Alongside Internal Teams and Partners 

An important characteristic of SAP Professional Services is that they are designed to work alongside both internal HR teams and existing partners, not replacing them. 

HR teams retain decision‑making authority and organizational context. Partners continue to play a vital role in long‑term delivery, support models, and local execution where appropriate. SAP brings focused delivery capacity, deep product expertise, and experience across many customer scenarios. Together, this collaborative model allows organizations to move forward faster without overextending internal resources or disrupting established partner relationships. 

Where Targeted Services Make the Biggest Difference 

In practice, HR teams most often draw on SAP Professional Services in scenarios where speed, quality, or risk management matter more than long‑term internal capability building. 

Post go-live optimization services are frequently used to refine processes as organizational needs become clearer. Rather than revisiting configuration incrementally, HR teams can leverage a structured engagement to improve efficiency, reduce manual work, or align released functionality with evolving business goals. 

Similarly, implementation or expansion services are often used when extending SAP SuccessFactors to new regions, user populations, or functional areas. These engagements allow HR teams to reuse internal and partner knowledge while relying on SAP expertise to execute specific configuration, integration, or data activities within a defined timeframe. 

Learning services also play an important role when change adoption becomes the bottleneck rather than technical setup. Supporting organizational change, enablement planning, and user training through a focused engagement can significantly shorten the gap between system availability and effective use. 

Complementing SAP Success Plans, Not Replacing Them 

It is important to distinguish SAP Professional Services from SAP Success Plans. 

SAP Success Plans define the ongoing engagement model: how SAP provides guidance, insight, and support over time. SAP Professional Services, on the other hand, deliver execution: they create tangible outputs through focused project work, whether that is configuration, integration, optimization, data migration, or enablement. 

Many customers use the two together: 

  • SAP Success Plans help HR teams identify where change would deliver value and reduce risk 
  • SAP Professional Services then provide the delivery capacity to act on those insights efficiently, often in collaboration with partners. 

This separation allows HR organizations to avoid a binary build‑versus‑buy mindset. Instead, they can retain strategic ownership internally while selectively buying execution when it accelerates outcomes. 

From an HR leadership perspective, accelerating time to value is not about doing everything faster. It is about being deliberate about where internal teams invest their time, where partners provide scale, and where targeted SAP expertise can unblock progress without compromising quality. 

SAP Professional Services support this approach by offering scenario‑based, non‑recurring engagements that can be scoped to immediate needs and aligned to the broader HR roadmap. Used in this way, they do not create dependency; they create momentum. 

How to Initiate a Professional Services Engagement 

Because SAP Professional Services are delivered under a separate services contract, the starting point is a scoping discussion. This allows HR and SAP to clarify the business objective, define deliverables, and agree on the most suitable service model: whether fixed scope or tailored, fixed price or time and materials. 

HR teams can initiate this conversation through their Customer Success Manager, Account Executive, or Services Sales Executive, depending on the existing engagement model.

entry.title: How to add Opportunity ID as a hyperlink column in Lead Level (COD_Mkt_Prospect) OWL screen
entry.content:
Hi Expert,

Background:
We are working on the COD_Mkt_Prospect OWL screen (Marketing Leads) in SAP Cloud Applications Studio (C4C SDK). Our requirement is to add a new column that displays the related Opportunity ID as a clickable hyperlink, so when a user clicks on it, it navigates directly to the Opportunity TI screen.

What we tried:

 

Opened the COD_Mkt_Prospect OWL in UI Designer
Clicked on various columns and entities in Extensibility Explorer
Clicked on PaneContainer, ButtonGroup, COD_Mkt_Prospect entity in Extensibility Explorer
The Operations section only shows "Widget Mashups Management" — no "Add Extension to List" or "Add View with Embedded Component" option is available

 

What we want to achieve:

Add a new column ZOpportunityID of type BusinessTransactionDocumentID to the OWL list
The column should render as a hyperlink — clicking it navigates to the Opportunity TI screen with the correct dynamic ID per row
Reference blog we followed:
We referred to this SAP blog — "Link to TI screen of another BO from standard OWL screen" — which covers a similar scenario for Sales Quote OWL. However, in our case the Extensibility Explorer does not show the required anchor/operations on the COD_Mkt_Prospect OWL screen.

Blog reference: https://blogs.sap.com/2019/11/29/link-to-ti-screen-of-another-bo-from-standard-owl-screen/

Our questions:

Is it possible to add a hyperlink column (Opportunity ID) to the COD_Mkt_Prospect OWL screen at all?
Why does the Extensibility Explorer show no anchor/operations on this OWL — is this a standard limitation of the Marketing Prospect screen?
Is there any alternative approach — such as using an Embedded Component with AdvancedListPane, or enhancing the screen via XBO — to achieve the same result?
Can the HTML Widget Mashup approach be used to show a dynamic Opportunity ID hyperlink per row in the OWL list?
Any guidance or alternative approach from the community would be very much appreciated please if any Alternative approched we have can you please tell Steps Or any Reference which we follow to Achive this type of Requirment. Thank you!

#C4 #Cloud Applications Studio  #OW #Embedded Componen #OBN Navigatio #Hyperlin
#Extensibility Explore, #SDK, @SAP , @SDK

entry.title: CX Onboarding - New Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2 Playlist on AI
entry.content:
New Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2 Playlist on AI: How AI Is Transforming Customer Experience

The business world is changing fast, and customer expectations are higher than ever. Today, customers expect seamless, personalized experiences at every touchpoint—and artificial intelligence is no longer a nice-to-have, but a fundamental capability to deliver them.

AI is transforming how organizations operate by enabling them to better understand customer behavior, anticipate needs, and respond with precision. What was once reactive and manual is becoming proactive and intelligent, allowing businesses to move faster, make better decisions, and create more meaningful connections with their customers.

At SAP, this transformation goes beyond isolated AI use cases. It is part of a broader vision: the Autonomous Enterprise. In this model, end-to-end business processes are powered by AI and organized across core functions such as Finance, Supply Chain, Procurement, Human Capital Management, and Customer Experience. AI agents operate across these domains, executing complex workflows autonomously while maintaining enterprise governance and control.

Within this vision, Customer Experience plays a central role. Autonomous CX brings together CRM and customer experience processes into a unified system, where AI acts on a complete, real-time view of your business. This enables organizations to deliver consistent, intelligent, and highly personalized experiences at scale.

This transformation becomes tangible in solutions like SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2, where AI is embedded directly into everyday workflows.

In customer service, AI helps teams move from information overload to instant expertise. Capabilities such as case management assistance streamline case preparation, processing, and resolution while learning from every interaction—turning expert knowledge into reusable insights for the entire team.

In sales, AI enables a shift from data overload to guided selling. Intelligent assistants help prioritize opportunities, identify risks early, and automate time-consuming tasks like quote generation, allowing sales teams to focus on what matters most: closing deals and building relationships.

Together, these capabilities represent a fundamental shift in how Customer Experience is delivered in both efficiency and better outcomes for customers.

As AI adoption continues to accelerate, many organizations are looking for practical ways to get started and realize value quickly—especially during onboarding and implementation phases.

To support our customers on this journey, the CX Onboarding team has created a 📺comprehensive video playlist for SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2. This playlist is designed to introduce the key AI capabilities available within the solutions and help you accelerate adoption, maximize ROI, and build a strong foundation for success.

👇Read below to learn more.

Introduction to SAP Business AI

Learn why AI is more important than ever and about SAP’s integrated approach to Customer Experience and intelligent applications. It shows how SAP connects CX with backend systems through a unified data layer, enabling real-time insights and seamless collaboration across business functions.

Joule, SAP’s Business Copilot

Get introduced to Joule, SAP’s AI copilot, and how it enables natural, conversational interaction with business systems. Joule helps users access information quickly, automate tasks, and navigate to the right applications, all while integrating with tools like Microsoft 365.

AI Agents for SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2

This video explains what AI agents are and how they operate autonomously to perform tasks with minimal human intervention. It showcases use cases such as automatic case classification and a Q&A agent that improves response accuracy and consistency.

Generative AI Features for SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2

This session focuses on generative AI and how it creates new content based on learned patterns. It demonstrates features such as Lead Booster, which enhances customer understanding, and the Email Draft Recommender, which helps streamline communication.

Machine Learning Features for SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2

Explore how machine learning helps systems learn from data and improve over time. Become familiar with its capabilities such as Lead Intelligence, which predicts conversion likelihood, and Case Topic Analyzer, which organizes case data into meaningful insights.

🔍Explore our playlist to learn how to make the most out of AI with Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Thank you for reading this blog. We hope you found the content insightful. Please give us a like or leave a comment; we’d love to hear from you!

You can request a one‑on‑one call with one of our onboarding advisors using this form. 

💻Want to get in touch with us? Send an email to SAP_CX_Onboarding@sap.com.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

👉Key Resources

  • Onboarding Resource Center: SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2
  • AI guide for Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2
  • Joule Agents and Joule Assistants for customer experience
  • SAP Business AI in the Discovery Center

entry.title: Impact Analysis (Change Record) – “Key is invalid” error for BOM in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 260
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community 🙏

We are working on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 and implementing Impact Analysis within Change Records (Engineering Change Management).  We are facing an issue when trying to execute an impact analysis on a BOM object.

Issue
When launching Impact Analysis from a Change Record with a BOM item, we receive the following error:
Key is invalid. Check the UI Field Mapping Customization.
or sometimes:
Object <Plant/Material/Alt> does not exist or you do not have the requisite authorization

Context
We are trying to perform impact analysis for a Bill of Material (BOM) using:

  • Object: BOM (S03)
  • CDS View: A_BILLOFMATERIAL_1

The Change Record contains a BOM with:

  • Material
  • Plant
  • BOM Usage (Production / Engineering)
  • Alternative BOM

Configuration done (CBC)

  • Impact Modeler Object Configuration
    • Object: BOM (S03)
    • CDS View: A_BILLOFMATERIAL_1
  • Analyzer Field Mapping

APPS "Manage Impact Analysis Models":

APPS "Impact Analysis"

APPS "Change Record":

 

Questions

  1. Does Impact Analysis with CDS A_BILLOFMATERIAL_1 require a technical BOM identifier (BillOfMaterial UUID) instead of the functional key (Material + Plant + Usage + Alternative)?
  2. Is there any standard limitation on BOM Usage (e.g. Engineering BOM not supported)?
  3. Is additional configuration required to map the BOM key correctly between Change Record UI and CDS view?
  4. Are there known issues or OSS notes related to this error in version 2602?

entry.title: Re: Early Warning System Set up (EWS) in SAP MM/WM
entry.content: @MignozziM Well, technically it is still there, i.e. the ABAP code is operational in S/4HANA. However Early Warning system doesn't work by itself. It is a part of Logistics Information System (LIS) that is being sunset in S/4, e.g. Sales Information System (SIS) is in a compatibility pack mode, Inventory Controlling (INVCO) is not compatible with parallel material documents postings, etc. Check corresponding support notes. LIS is demode now, taken over by embedded analytics based on CDS views.

entry.title: Building a Native MCP Server in SAP ABAP: Enabling AI Agents to Talk to Your SAP System
entry.content:
Hi All!

The content of this blog was initially written in Chinese by myself (Not AI), but later it was translated into English with the help of AI, and the AI also enhanced and structured the content. And the content reflects my own experiences and thoughts, and includes both text and images.


I'm a bit of a newcomer when it comes to posting here — but definitely not a stranger to here. I've been lurking and learning from your blogs for over a decade, and countless threads here have saved me in real-world projects. So thank you all!

Recently, I built something that genuinely excited me, and I wanted to share it with this community. This post is about how I explored AI Agents, connected them with SAP, and ultimately built a fully custom MCP Server natively inside SAP ABAP — enabling AI Agents to interact directly with SAP business processes using natural language.

 

Background: The Mission

Back in early January this year, my team lead gave me an open-ended challenge: while continuing to support our current work, explore how AI can drive business innovation.

I dove in. Here's what I explored:

  • Set up a local AI model (Qwen/Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) on my Windows PC refering the ModelScope.
  • Built a simple Python-based Chat Agent with VS Code extensions (Lingma/Copilot)
  • Created a local MCP Server (Python-based using Lingma/Copilot) with tools for things like: checking GPU status, logging into SAP, downloading email attachments from Outlook, detecting invoice files and auto-sorting them

The result: I could have natural language conversations with the AI, and it would intelligently call these tools to get things done. Pretty cool!

 

The First Breakthrough — and the Problem It Revealed

After building the local MCP Server, I started thinking: what if I could make the AI Agent interact directly with SAP?

I added a tool to my local MCP Server that called an SAP API. It worked! The AI Agent could now talk to SAP. I was thrilled.

But then reality hit.

Every single SAP API endpoint required:

  1. Creating a new API endpoint on the SAP side
  2. Adding a new tool in the Python MCP Server to call that endpoint
  3. Manually handling and exposing all the input parameters

Scale that to hundreds or thousands of business scenarios, and the maintenance burden would be enormous. Worse, the tight coupling between the Python MCP Server and the SAP system felt architecturally wrong. I wanted something more elegant — a decoupled, generalized framework.

So the question became: What if I built the MCP Server directly inside SAP?

 

The Solution: A Native SAP ABAP MCP Server

Learning MCP First

Before building anything, I went back to basics. I studied the Model Context Protocol (MCP) from the official documentation. And combined with video tutorials, I got a clear picture of how MCP works: it's a standard protocol that defines how AI Agents discover and call tools, enabling LLMs to interact with external systems in a structured way.

System Architecture

Here's the high-level architecture I designed:

All MCP interactions — initialization, tool listing, and tool invocation — go through a single SAP ICF endpoint. The SAP system itself acts as the MCP Server.

 

Key ABAP Components

Here is a breakdown of the core classes and their roles:

The ICF HTTP Handler Class

Registered as an ICF service in SICF, this class is the entry point. It receives all incoming HTTP requests from the AI Agent and delegates them to the MCP Server.

The Core MCP Server Class

This is the brain. It handles:

  • Tool registration (In CONSTRUCTOR via the Tool Registry Class)
  • Request parsing 
  • MCP Initialization handshake 
  • Tool listing 
  • Tool invocation
  • JSON response building for the AI Agent

The Tool Registry Class

A central registry where all MCP tools are registered. When the MCP Server initializes, it queries this registry to build the tool catalog that gets exposed to AI Agents.

The Tool Interface

A standard ABAP interface that every tool class must implement. It defines:

  • Tool metadata: name, description (sent to the LLM so it knows when to use this tool)
  • Input schema: parameter definitions (name, type, description, required flag)
  • Execution logic: how to process the AI Agent's request and return a result

A Demo Tool Class

A sample tool that queries customer data. It demonstrates the full pattern:

  1. Define tool name and description
  2. Define input schema 
  3. Receive input from the AI Agent, parse it into ABAP fields
  4. Execute ABAP business logic
  5. Serialize result as JSON and return it up the chain: MCP Server → HTTP Handler → Agent → LLM → User

Key Design Principle: One Endpoint to Rule Them All

The most important design decision here is that the entire MCP protocol is served through a single ICF endpoint. Whether the AI Agent is initializing, requesting the tool list, or calling a specific tool — it all goes to the same URL. The MCP Server internally routes based on the method name in the request payload.

This dramatically simplifies the SAP-side setup. Adding a new business tool means:

  1. Create a new ABAP class implementing the tool interface.
  2. Register it in configuration table.
  3. Done — no new ICF services, no new HTTP configurations.

💡The LLM automatically discovers all registered tools through the tool listing handshake. You define the tool's description carefully, and the model figures out when to call it.

Funny work: You can try to use AI coding tools (Lingma/Copilot) to help write ABAP classes based on the Key ABAP Components part.

 

Results & Demo

By late January / early February, I had a fully functional, self-built SAP MCP Server running in our SAP system. I demoed it to the team, and in the following two months I refined it further, while also exploring broader Agent application topics like RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation), Skills, and enterprise-grade Agent frameworks.

For demo screenshots showing real AI Agent ↔ SAP interactions, please refer the belows:

 

AI Agent Applications Used for Testing

I used existing AI Agent products as the client-side interface — no custom frontend needed:

  • GitHub Copilot CLI
  • Dify Platform
  • Codebuddy CLI
  • AnythingLLM

All of these support the MCP protocol, so they connect to the SAP MCP Server out of the box. Download and install any of them to try this yourself.

 

 

What's Next?

This is just the beginning. Some areas I'm actively exploring:

  • Expanding the tool library with more business-relevant SAP tools
  • Connecting multiple SAP systems through a federated MCP Server pattern
  • Integrating RAG to give the AI Agent access to SAP documentation and business knowledge

 

Closing Thoughts

I built this because I wanted to prove that SAP and modern AI Agents can work together natively — without heavy middleware or expensive third-party platforms. The MCP standard makes this surprisingly achievable even from a pure ABAP background.

If you're an ABAPer curious about AI, or an AI enthusiast wondering how to connect LLMs to SAP — I hope this inspires you to experiment. The barrier is lower than you think.

Would love to hear your thoughts, questions, or ideas in the comments. Let's explore this together! 🚀
